Rita sets her sights on the Green Ranger power she originally weaponized, plotting to drain it from its new bearer. Her cunning forces the Rangers onto the defensive.

The arc establishes Rita as a thinking villain whose schemes target the team's emotional and magical vulnerabilities alike.

relationships.summary: The hero Rita first corrupted and now seeks to destroy.
relationships.notes: Rita's manipulation is the origin of Tommy's lasting guilt.

media.title: Mighty Morphin Power Rangers
media.notes: The original incarnation of Rita Repulsa.

media.title: Power Rangers
media.notes: The 2017 reboot film featured a reimagined Rita Repulsa.

entities.name: Robot
entities.short_bio: Robot is a brilliant strategist who operates through a drone body controlled from a distance, hiding a complicated truth about his real form. He is instrumental in reforming the Guardians, though his methods are morally ambiguous.

eras.title: The Architect
eras.summary: Robot's intellect drives the rebuilding of Earth's defenses, even as his ethics blur.

arcs.title: Building a Better Team
arcs.logline: A mind without equal sets out to engineer the perfect defense for the world.
arcs.summary:
After the collapse of the original Guardians, Robot takes a leading role in reconstituting the team. His tactical genius and resources make him indispensable, but his willingness to bend rules raises questions.

His arc traces the line between heroic ambition and dangerous overreach, as he pursues an idealized vision of safety that not everyone trusts.

reading_entries.title: Invincible, Vol. 4: Head of the Class

start_here.label: Absolute Beginner
start_here.reason: Watch Robot engineer the reformed Guardians from the ground up.

relationships.summary: Robot leads the reformation of the Guardians and serves as its chief strategist.
relationships.notes: His intellect rebuilds the team, though his methods remain controversial.

media.title: Invincible
media.notes: A key member of the reformed team.

media.title: Invincible VS
media.notes: Appears in the fighting-game roster.

entities.name: Rogue
entities.short_bio: Rogue is a mutant who absorbs the memories, abilities, and life force of anyone she touches, a power that long made physical contact dangerous. Once a member of the Brotherhood, she reformed and became one of the X-Men's most beloved and dependable members. Her struggle for control and connection makes her one of the franchise's most relatable heroes.

eras.title: From Brotherhood to X-Men
eras.summary: Rogue's journey from villain to hero, and her ongoing battle to control her absorbing power, made her a fan favorite across the modern era.

arcs.title: Seeking Sanctuary
arcs.logline: A former villain begs her enemies for help controlling the power that isolates her.
arcs.summary:
Overwhelmed by the personalities and powers she has absorbed, Rogue turns to Professor Xavier for help, abandoning the Brotherhood. The X-Men's wary acceptance begins her path to redemption.

Her arc explores trust, loneliness, and the longing for human connection denied by her own gift.

It transforms a dangerous adversary into one of the team's most loyal members.

start_here.label: A villain's redemption
start_here.reason: Rogue's defection from the Brotherhood is one of the franchise's great character turns.

relationships.summary: Rogue reformed to become a stalwart member and frequent leader of the X-Men.
relationships.notes: Her redemption arc is a cornerstone of the modern team.

media.title: X-Men
media.notes: Anna Paquin plays Rogue in the original film trilogy.

media.title: X-Men: The Animated Series
media.notes: A central and popular member of the animated team.

media.title: X-Men '97
media.notes: A lead character with major emotional arcs in the revival.

entities.name: Rupert Giles
entities.short_bio: Rupert Giles is Buffy's Watcher and the Sunnydale High librarian, a scholar of the occult who trains and guides the Slayer. His vast knowledge and quiet authority make him the gang's indispensable mentor.

eras.title: The Watcher
eras.summary: Giles takes up his charge to train Buffy, balancing duty to the Watchers' Council against genuine care for his Slayer.

arcs.title: Duty and Devotion
arcs.logline: A reserved librarian must shape a reluctant girl into the world's last defense.
arcs.summary:
Giles arrives in Sunnydale to fulfill the role he was raised for: guiding the Slayer. He brings centuries of accumulated lore and the rigid expectations of the Watchers' Council, only to find Buffy challenging both.

As the threats mount, Giles learns to temper duty with compassion, becoming less a handler and more a father figure. His arc is about the cost and reward of devotion to a calling and to a person.

reading_entries.title: Buffy the Vampire Slayer Vol. 1: High School Is Hell

start_here.label: Absolute Beginner
start_here.reason: Giles's introduction establishes the Watcher mythology and his mentorship of Buffy.

relationships.summary: Buffy's Watcher and mentor, supplying the lore and discipline her calling demands.
relationships.notes: Giles trains and steadies Buffy, serving as a father figure throughout her tenure as Slayer.

media.title: Buffy the Vampire Slayer
media.notes: Giles serves as Buffy's Watcher across the series.

media.title: Buffy the Vampire Slayer
media.notes: Giles supports Buffy with guidance throughout the game.

entities.name: Sabretooth
entities.short_bio: Victor Creed is a feral mutant killer who shares Wolverine's healing factor, heightened senses, and savage instincts, but none of his restraint. A merciless assassin whose past is entangled with Logan's, Sabretooth is the dark reflection of what Wolverine could have become.

eras.title: The Savage Rival
eras.summary: From a generic martial-arts villain into Wolverine's defining nemesis, Creed becomes the predator who haunts Logan's life and memories.

arcs.title: Mutant Massacre
arcs.logline: Sabretooth joins a death squad in a slaughter that scars the X-Men forever.
arcs.summary:
As a member of Mister Sinister's Marauders, Sabretooth takes part in the brutal massacre of the underground Morlock community, establishing him as a genuine monster rather than a mere brawler.

The crossover raises the character's profile across the X-line and seeds his future role as Wolverine's most personal enemy. Creed's glee in the carnage makes the threat unforgettable.

It is the moment Sabretooth graduates into the upper tier of X-Men villains.

start_here.label: Best Introduction
start_here.reason: Mutant Massacre shows exactly how dangerous Sabretooth is and why his shadow falls so heavily over Wolverine.

relationships.summary: Sabretooth is Wolverine's savage opposite and most personal nemesis.
relationships.notes: Their blood feud spans decades; Creed delights in killing the people Logan loves and claims a shared, manipulated past.

media.title: X-Men Origins: Wolverine
media.notes: Liev Schreiber plays Victor Creed as Logan's half-brother in a featured antagonist role.

media.title: X-Men: The Animated Series
media.notes: A recurring villain and member of Magneto's and Apocalypse's forces across the series.

media.title: Marvel vs. Capcom 2
media.notes: A playable fighter showcasing his ferocity and speed.

entities.name: Sam and Twitch
entities.short_bio: Sam Burke and Twitch Williams are NYPD homicide detectives whose dogged investigations keep dragging them into Spawn's supernatural world. The mismatched partners became popular enough to earn their own long-running spinoff series.

eras.title: The Detectives
eras.summary: The two detectives work the violent streets where Spawn operates, gradually uncovering the supernatural beneath the city's crimes.

arcs.title: Udaku
arcs.logline: Two detectives chase a case that pulls them past the edge of the natural world.
arcs.summary:
Sam and Twitch pursue a string of killings that resist ordinary explanation, leading them toward the supernatural forces stalking the city. Their grounded police work contrasts with the cosmic stakes around them.

The arc showcases the partners' chemistry and establishes them as the human lens through which Spawn's world is seen.

start_here.label: Absolute Beginner
start_here.reason: Udaku is the definitive showcase of the detective duo.

relationships.summary: The detectives repeatedly cross paths with Spawn, sometimes aiding his war.
relationships.notes: Their investigations intersect with Spawn's crusade against street-level evil.

media.title: Todd McFarlane's Spawn
media.notes: Featured as recurring detectives.

media.title: Spawn
media.notes: Twitch appears in the live-action adaptation.

entities.name: Sandman
entities.short_bio: Small-time crook Flint Marko was caught in a nuclear test blast that fused his body with irradiated sand, granting him the power to transform into living, shape-shifting sand. As the Sandman he can become a sledgehammer fist, a swirling sandstorm, or slip away through the smallest crack. A recurring early foe, he has drifted between hardened criminal and reluctant antihero over the decades.

eras.title: The Shifting Menace
eras.summary: The Sandman establishes himself as one of Spider-Man's earliest powered foes and a founding threat among his team-up villains.

arcs.title: The Sandman Strikes
arcs.logline: A burglar who can turn to sand becomes one of Spider-Man's most slippery enemies.
arcs.summary:
After gaining his sand-transforming powers, Flint Marko launches a one-man crime wave that Spider-Man struggles to contain, since his foe can simply pour himself through bars and reform unharmed. The wall-crawler must outthink rather than overpower him.

The Sandman quickly becomes a fixture of Spider-Man's rogues' gallery, joining forces with other villains in the Sinister Six and the Frightful Four, and his elemental powers make him a memorable recurring threat across the hero's career.

reading_entries.title: Marvel Masterworks: The Amazing Spider-Man Vol. 1

start_here.label: Classic Fan
start_here.reason: Meet one of Spider-Man's earliest super-powered foes and a charter member of the Sinister Six.

relationships.summary: Sandman is a recurring early foe whose shape-shifting sand powers make him hard to defeat.
relationships.notes: Flint Marko debuted in Amazing Spider-Man #4 and became a founding member of the Sinister Six, repeatedly clashing with Spider-Man over the decades.

media.title: Spider-Man 3
media.notes: Thomas Haden Church portrays a sympathetic Sandman in Sam Raimi's film.

media.title: Spider-Man: The Animated Series
media.notes: Appears as a recurring elemental villain.

media.title: Spider-Man 3: The Game
media.notes: Featured as a boss based on the film incarnation.

entities.name: Savage Dragon
entities.real_name: Dragon (later "Malcolm Dragon" era; the original is simply "Dragon")
entities.short_bio: A massive, fin-headed amnesiac with superhuman strength and a rapid healing factor, Dragon woke in a burning Chicago field with no memory of his past and chose to serve as a police officer fighting the city's superpowered crime wave. Created by Erik Larsen, the series later passes the mantle to his son Malcolm, making it one of comics' longest-running creator-owned superhero sagas.

eras.title: Officer Dragon Era
eras.summary: The original Dragon emerges with no memory and becomes a Chicago cop, battling the city's organized superhuman crime and uncovering pieces of his lost origin. This long stretch defines the brutal, soap-operatic, and relentlessly forward-moving tone of the series.

arcs.title: A Force to Be Reckoned With
arcs.logline: An amnesiac green-skinned powerhouse is recruited into the Chicago Police Department to fight super-criminals.
arcs.summary:
Dragon is found unconscious and badly burned in a Chicago field, with no memory of who he is or how he got there. A police lieutenant convinces him to use his immense strength and durability in service of the law, and he reluctantly joins the force at a moment when the city is overrun by super-powered gangs answering to a shadowy crime lord.

The early issues establish the book's defining mix: street-level police procedural crossed with larger-than-life super-brawls. Dragon proves nearly unstoppable in a fight, but the series quickly shows that victories come at a cost, and that the people around him are as important to the story as the punches.

This origin arc lays the groundwork for the central mystery of Dragon's identity while introducing the supporting cast, the precinct, and the recurring villains who will define his career.

reading_entries.title: Savage Dragon: A Force to Be Reckoned With

arcs.title: The Fallen
arcs.logline: Dragon's career as Chicago's premier hero is tested by escalating threats and personal loss.
arcs.summary:
As Dragon settles into his role, the threats grow larger and more personal. The criminal underworld reorganizes under new leadership, and Dragon's status as the city's most visible hero makes him a target for those who want to prove themselves by taking him down.

The arc deepens the supporting cast and pushes Dragon's relationships into more dangerous territory, balancing explosive action with the ongoing soap-opera threads that keep readers invested between the fights.

It also continues to peel back layers of Dragon's mysterious origin, hinting that his amnesia conceals a past far darker and stranger than a simple accident.

eras.title: Malcolm Dragon Era
eras.summary: The series passes its lead role to Dragon's son Malcolm, who inherits his father's powers and his place in a changed, often hostile world. This era reframes the saga around a younger generation, family life, and a hero trying to do right while the public turns against superhumans.

arcs.title: A New Dragon
arcs.logline: Malcolm Dragon steps up to protect his city and family as the new face of the series.
arcs.summary:
With his father's story reaching a turning point, Malcolm Dragon takes center stage. Younger, more impulsive, and navigating the ordinary chaos of relationships and school alongside super-heroics, Malcolm brings a fresh voice to a long-running book.

The arc balances generational change with continuity, keeping the brutal action and ongoing soap-opera structure intact while shifting the emotional core to a new protagonist learning what it means to carry the Dragon name.

Malcolm must contend with both inherited enemies and new threats, all while the world around him grows increasingly suspicious of people with powers.

arcs.title: Public Enemy
arcs.logline: As anti-superhuman sentiment boils over, Malcolm finds himself fighting both villains and a fearful public.
arcs.summary:
Malcolm's heroism collides with a rising tide of public fear and political hostility toward superhumans. What once made Dragon a celebrated protector now makes his family a lightning rod for suspicion and persecution.

The arc leans into social and political tension, putting Malcolm in situations where punching the bad guy is not enough and the real conflict is with institutions and prejudice. His family life and responsibilities raise the personal stakes considerably.

Through it all, Malcolm tries to hold onto the idealism that defines him, even as the world gives him fewer and fewer reasons to.

start_here.label: Absolute Beginner
start_here.reason: Start at the very beginning to meet the amnesiac Dragon and watch his origin unfold from issue one.

start_here.label: Classic Fan
start_here.reason: The Fallen captures peak original-Dragon action and the soap-opera intensity longtime readers love.

start_here.label: Modern Era
start_here.reason: A New Dragon is the natural jumping-on point for the Malcolm-led modern run.

relationships.summary: Dragon's police partner and one of his closest personal connections on the force.
relationships.notes: Alex Wilde works alongside Dragon at the precinct, and their bond becomes one of the emotional anchors of his early career.

relationships.summary: The police lieutenant who first recruited Dragon and championed him within the department.
relationships.notes: Frank Darling's faith in Dragon gives the amnesiac newcomer a place and a purpose in Chicago.

relationships.summary: A super-strong hero who fights alongside Dragon as a trusted teammate.
relationships.notes: Barbaric and his partner often back Dragon up against threats too big for one hero to handle.

relationships.summary: A fast, agile hero and frequent ally during Dragon's larger super-team engagements.
relationships.notes: Ricochet's speed complements Dragon's brute force in their shared battles.

relationships.summary: The armored crime lord who rules Chicago's superhuman underworld and Dragon's foundational nemesis.
relationships.notes: Overlord's grip on the city's organized crime makes him the defining antagonist of Dragon's earliest cases.

relationships.summary: A monstrous, scheming villain who repeatedly seizes power and torments Dragon across decades.
relationships.notes: Cyberface evolves from underling to world-conquering threat, becoming one of Dragon's most persistent enemies.

relationships.summary: A vicious, recurring foe whose brutality tests Dragon at his limits.
relationships.notes: The Fiend embodies the savage, no-holds-barred violence that defines the series' rogues.

relationships.summary: A diminutive alien mastermind with grand, world-threatening ambitions.
relationships.notes: Mister Glum's schemes from another dimension draw Dragon into conflicts far beyond Chicago's streets.

media.title: Savage Dragon
media.notes: An animated series adaptation that aired as part of the USA Action Extreme Team block.

media.title: Savage Dragon: The Game
media.notes: A mobile game based on the comic series.

media.title: Savage Dragon (handheld)
media.notes: A handheld electronic game tie-in produced during the character's 1990s peak.

entities.name: Sentinel
entities.short_bio: Sentinel is an armored operative who bridges the classic and modern incarnations of the team. Disciplined and tech-driven, he represents Youngblood's evolution into newer eras.

eras.title: New Guard
eras.summary: Sentinel carries the team forward through its modern relaunches.

arcs.title: Standing Watch
arcs.logline: An armored newcomer steps in to modernize the team's defensive line.
arcs.summary:
Sentinel joins as the team reinvents itself for a new era, bringing advanced tech and a steady temperament. His arc bridges legacy members and fresh recruits.

He embodies the franchise's push toward sharper, more contemporary storytelling.

relationships.summary: Sentinel is a member of Youngblood across modern eras.
relationships.notes: He bridges the team's classic and contemporary rosters.

media.title: Youngblood (Sega CD prototype era)
media.notes: Appears as a defensive specialist.

media.title: Youngblood (proposed animated adaptation)
media.notes: Considered for modern adaptations.

entities.name: Shaft
entities.short_bio: Shaft is the bow-wielding field leader of Youngblood's home team and the franchise's signature hero. A disciplined marksman, he balances genuine heroism against the demands of celebrity and government oversight.

eras.title: Founding Leader
eras.summary: Shaft anchors the original team as its public face and tactical leader.

arcs.title: Takes Aim
arcs.logline: A celebrated archer steps up to lead a government-backed superteam in the spotlight.
arcs.summary:
Shaft establishes himself as the steady center of a flashy, media-managed squad. His leadership is tested by both external threats and the pressures of fame.

The arc defines his blend of professionalism and idealism, setting the tone for the franchise.

start_here.label: Absolute Beginner
start_here.reason: Meet the team's leader at the franchise's origin.

relationships.summary: Shaft leads Youngblood's home team.
relationships.notes: He is the squad's public face and tactical commander.

media.title: Youngblood (Sega CD prototype era)
media.notes: Featured as a playable team leader.

media.title: Youngblood (developmental feature)
media.notes: Central to proposed adaptations.

entities.name: Sinestro
entities.short_bio: Once the greatest Green Lantern of them all, Thaal Sinestro of Korugar believed order was worth any price and became a tyrant over his own people. Stripped of his ring and exiled, he forged a yellow ring of fear and built a Corps in his own image. He is Hal Jordan's archenemy and the dark mirror of everything a Lantern can become.

eras.title: The Renegade Lantern
eras.summary: Sinestro's transformation from disgraced Lantern to wielder of the yellow ring of fear, the recurring nemesis of Hal Jordan.

arcs.title: The Yellow Ring
arcs.logline: A fallen hero returns from the antimatter universe armed with fear.
arcs.summary:
Banished to the antimatter universe of Qward for his tyranny, Sinestro is gifted a yellow power ring by the Weaponers, exploiting the impurity that renders Green Lanterns vulnerable to the color yellow. He returns to torment Hal Jordan and the Corps that cast him out.

The story establishes the fear-versus-will dynamic that defines the franchise, casting Sinestro as the discipline of order taken to a monstrous extreme. His knowledge of Corps tactics makes him uniquely dangerous.

reading_entries.title: Green Lantern: The Silver Age Omnibus Vol. 1

eras.title: The Sinestro Corps
eras.summary: Sinestro assembles an army of the universe's most terrifying beings to wage war on the Green Lantern Corps.

arcs.title: Sinestro Corps Rising
arcs.logline: Fear becomes an army, and Oa burns.
arcs.summary:
Sinestro recruits the most fear-inspiring entities across the cosmos and arms them with yellow rings, forming the Sinestro Corps. Allied with Parallax and the Anti-Monitor, he launches a devastating assault on Oa intended to break the Green Lantern Corps forever.

The war forces the Guardians to authorize lethal force for the first time, reshaping the Corps and elevating Sinestro into one of DC's premier cosmic villains.

reading_entries.title: Green Lantern: The Sinestro Corps War

start_here.label: Best Introduction
start_here.reason: The Sinestro Corps War is the definitive Sinestro story, showing the full threat of his fear-powered army against the Green Lantern Corps.

relationships.summary: Sinestro is Hal Jordan's archenemy and the dark reflection of the Green Lantern ideal.
relationships.notes: Their rivalry began in Green Lantern #7 (1961) and peaked in the Sinestro Corps War, a generational struggle between will and fear.

media.title: Green Lantern
media.notes: Mark Strong plays Sinestro, a Corps leader whose turn toward fear is set up for a sequel that never came.

media.title: Green Lantern: The Animated Series
media.notes: Sinestro appears as a Green Lantern whose ambitions foreshadow his fall.

media.title: Injustice 2
media.notes: Sinestro is a playable fighter wielding the yellow ring of fear.

entities.name: Spawn
entities.short_bio: Al Simmons, a murdered government assassin, strikes a bargain with a demon lord to return to the living and is reborn as a Hellspawn bound to a sentient symbiotic costume. Caught between Heaven and Hell, he wages a lonely war in the alleys of New York City while clinging to the last shreds of his humanity.

eras.title: The McFarlane Foundations
eras.summary: Todd McFarlane launches Spawn as a founding Image title, establishing Al Simmons' Faustian origin and his grim crusade through the back alleys of New York. The early years build the cosmology of Heaven, Hell, and the Hellspawn caught between them.

arcs.title: Questions of the Damned
arcs.logline: A murdered assassin claws his way back from Hell only to discover the terrible price of his return.
arcs.summary:
Al Simmons wakes in a dark alley with no memory of how he died, his body burned and disfigured beneath a living costume he does not understand. As fragments of his former life surface, he realizes he has been gone for five years and that the woman he loved has moved on without him.

The early issues follow Spawn as he stumbles through the rules of his new existence, learning that his powers draw on a finite reserve of necroplasm and that every use brings him closer to a reckoning. A clownish, grotesque figure called the Violator appears as a tormentor and supposed guide, taunting Al with hints about the bargain he made.

This origin arc grounds the series in tragedy rather than triumph, framing Spawn less as a superhero than as a damned soul searching for meaning. It sets the tone for everything that follows.

arcs.title: The Violator Unleashed
arcs.logline: Hell sends its enforcer to drag the reluctant Hellspawn into the war he was made to fight.
arcs.summary:
The demon Violator escalates his campaign against Spawn, shedding his human disguise to reveal a monstrous true form. Their clashes carry Spawn deeper into the politics of Hell and the role Malebolgia intends for him as a general in the coming war.

As Spawn resists being weaponized, he begins to test the limits of his abilities and the costume's will. The conflict forces him to choose whether to embrace the power he has been given or fight against the master who granted it.

The arc cements the Violator as Spawn's signature foe and deepens the central tension between Al's lingering conscience and the infernal purpose stitched into his being.

eras.title: The Modern Crusade
eras.summary: Spawn endures past record-setting issue counts, reinventing himself across new mythologies, new hosts, and a sprawling shared universe. The modern era expands the franchise with team books and a darker, more cosmic reach.

arcs.title: Endgame
arcs.logline: Spawn abandons his alleys for a bold new status quo that redefines who and what he is.
arcs.summary:
After years of war, Spawn's story is reset by a sweeping shift that strips away old certainties and reframes his powers and purpose. The arc moves him out of the shadows and toward a larger confrontation with the forces that created him.

New creative voices reshape the character while honoring his tragic core, introducing questions about identity, control, and what remains of Al Simmons. The stakes broaden from personal vengeance to something approaching apocalypse.

This run revitalized the long-running series and laid groundwork for the expanded Spawn universe that followed.

arcs.title: The Scorched Assembled
arcs.logline: Spawn gathers a team of damned and divine warriors to face threats no single Hellspawn can stop.
arcs.summary:
In the expanded Spawn universe, Al Simmons assembles a strike team drawn from across the cosmology, pulling together allies and uneasy partners into a unit built to confront supernatural catastrophe. The team format opens the world beyond Spawn's solitary war.

New threats test the group's cohesion and force Spawn to lead rather than brood alone. The arc balances large-scale spectacle with the personal grudges that have always defined the character.

It represents the franchise's push into a connected universe of titles, with Spawn at its center as both founder and reluctant commander.

start_here.label: Absolute Beginner
start_here.reason: The origin arc introduces Al Simmons, his deal with Hell, and the rules of his new existence with no prior knowledge required.

start_here.label: Classic Fan
start_here.reason: The Violator's arrival defines the classic McFarlane-era conflict and Spawn's signature nemesis.

start_here.label: Modern Era
start_here.reason: The Scorched arc shows Spawn as the anchor of a modern connected universe of titles.

relationships.summary: A weathered old Hellspawn who mentors Al in the nature of his powers and the war he was made for.
relationships.notes: Cogliostro appears early in Spawn's existence as a guide, revealing hard truths about necroplasm and the cost of the bargain.

relationships.summary: Two NYPD homicide detectives whose investigations repeatedly cross paths with Spawn's war on the streets.
relationships.notes: Sam Burke and Twitch Williams orbit Spawn's world, occasionally aiding him and earning their own spinoff series.

relationships.summary: Al Simmons' best friend from his former life, now married to the woman Al left behind.
relationships.notes: Terry's safety becomes a recurring concern, and his unknowing connection to Al's past drives much of the early emotional stakes.

relationships.summary: A witch with ties to the supernatural underworld who becomes a key ally in Spawn's later crusades.
relationships.notes: Nyx lends magical expertise and fights alongside Spawn against threats that span Heaven and Hell.

relationships.summary: A grotesque demon and Hell's enforcer, sent to torment Spawn and bend him to Malebolgia's will.
relationships.notes: The Violator, often disguised as the clownish Clown, is Spawn's most persistent and personal foe.

relationships.summary: The demon lord who struck the bargain that made Al into a Hellspawn and commands him from the depths of Hell.
relationships.notes: Malebolgia is the architect of Spawn's damnation and the ultimate target of his rebellion.

relationships.summary: The corrupt government spymaster responsible for ordering Al Simmons' murder.
relationships.notes: Wynn's betrayal set Spawn's tragedy in motion, making him a human target for Al's earthly vengeance.

relationships.summary: A Heaven-aligned counterpart created to destroy the Hellspawn, mirroring Spawn's powers with holy purpose.
relationships.notes: The Anti-Spawn (Redeemer) embodies the war between Heaven and Hell turned directly against Al.

media.title: Todd McFarlane's Spawn
media.notes: Mature animated series that earned an Emmy and adapted early storylines.

media.title: Spawn
media.notes: Live-action feature starring Michael Jai White as Al Simmons.

media.title: Spawn: In the Demon's Hand
media.notes: Arena combat game featuring Spawn and supporting cast.

entities.name: Spider-Man
entities.short_bio: Bitten by a radioactive spider, science-whiz teenager Peter Parker gained wall-crawling strength, lightning reflexes, and a danger-sensing 'spider-sense.' After a personal tragedy taught him that with great power comes great responsibility, he became the wisecracking, perpetually-overworked hero of New York City. Spider-Man balances the everyday struggles of rent, romance, and a demanding day job with the constant duty of protecting the people around him.

eras.title: The Silver Age: Birth of a Hero
eras.summary: Stan Lee and Steve Ditko introduce a radically new kind of superhero: a guilt-ridden teenager whose powers complicate rather than solve his life. These foundational years establish Peter Parker's supporting cast, his rogues' gallery, and the moral code that defines him.

arcs.title: With Great Power
arcs.logline: A bullied science student gains spider powers and learns the hardest lesson of his life.
arcs.summary:
High school outcast Peter Parker is bitten by a radioactive spider during a science demonstration and discovers he has gained superhuman strength, agility, the ability to cling to walls, and a precognitive 'spider-sense.' Dazzled by the possibilities, Peter first uses his abilities for personal gain, designing a costume and web-shooters to break into show business as a television sensation.

When he selfishly lets a fleeing burglar escape, believing it none of his business, that same criminal later murders his beloved Uncle Ben. Confronting the killer, Peter is shattered to recognize the man he could have stopped. The tragedy crystallizes the principle that will guide the rest of his life.

This short, perfectly-formed origin story is one of the most reprinted and influential single issues in comics history, reframing the superhero as a flawed, relatable everyman.
arcs.spoiler: Uncle Ben is killed by the burglar Peter failed to stop, teaching him that with great power must come great responsibility.

reading_entries.title: Marvel Masterworks: The Amazing Spider-Man Vol. 1

arcs.title: The Master Planner Saga
arcs.logline: To save a dying loved one, Spider-Man must lift an impossible weight from his own shoulders.
arcs.summary:
When Aunt May falls gravely ill from a blood condition caused by an earlier transfusion of Peter's irradiated blood, the only cure is a rare serum stolen by the mysterious crime boss known as the Master Planner, who is secretly Doctor Octopus. Racing against time, an exhausted Spider-Man tears through the underworld to find it.

The saga culminates in the legendary sequence where Spider-Man, trapped beneath tons of collapsed machinery in a flooding underwater lab with the life-saving serum just out of reach, summons every ounce of his will to lift the wreckage off his back. It remains one of the most iconic depictions of heroic perseverance ever drawn.

Ditko's final great Spider-Man story, it distills the character's core appeal: not the powers, but the refusal to quit when everything is on the line.

reading_entries.title: Marvel Masterworks: The Amazing Spider-Man Vol. 4

eras.title: The Bronze Age: Loss and Legend
eras.summary: Spider-Man matures into adulthood and college life as the stories grow darker and more emotionally ambitious. This era contains some of the most consequential and shocking moments in the character's history, redefining what a superhero comic could risk.

arcs.title: The Night Gwen Stacy Died
arcs.logline: The Green Goblin learns Spider-Man's identity and strikes at the person he loves most.
arcs.summary:
Norman Osborn, having recovered the memories that reveal Spider-Man is Peter Parker, descends into full madness as the Green Goblin and abducts Peter's girlfriend Gwen Stacy. He lures Spider-Man to the top of a bridge for a final, vicious confrontation high above the river.

What follows is widely regarded as the moment the Silver Age of comics ended and a more unflinching era began. The story's devastating outcome proved that even the hero's greatest efforts could fail, and that loss in these stories could be permanent and real.

The two-issue arc reverberates through every Spider-Man story that follows, cementing the Green Goblin as his ultimate nemesis and haunting Peter for decades of publication.
arcs.spoiler: Gwen Stacy dies during the rescue attempt, a death many attribute to the whiplash of Spider-Man's own web-line; the Green Goblin is seemingly killed by his own glider.

reading_entries.title: Marvel Masterworks: The Amazing Spider-Man Vol. 13

arcs.title: Kraven's Last Hunt
arcs.logline: An aging hunter buries Spider-Man alive to prove he can become the better spider.
arcs.summary:
Kraven the Hunter, obsessed with finally conquering his white whale, shoots Spider-Man, buries him in a grave, and dons the hero's costume to prowl the city himself. Convinced he has at last defeated his prey, Kraven sets out to prove he can be a superior Spider-Man by brutalizing criminals the way he believes the real hero should.

Meanwhile, the buried Peter Parker must claw his way back from a literal grave, his psyche pushed to the brink. The story interweaves dread, obsession, and resurrection with a poetic, almost gothic tone unusual for the genre.

Written by J.M. DeMatteis with art by Mike Zeck, this self-contained tale is consistently ranked among the greatest Spider-Man stories ever told and a high point of mature superhero storytelling.

reading_entries.title: Spider-Man: Kraven's Last Hunt
reading_entries.issue_range: Web of Spider-Man #31-32, Amazing Spider-Man #293-294, Spectacular Spider-Man #131-132

eras.title: The Modern Age: A Hero for Every Reader
eras.summary: Spider-Man becomes a global multimedia icon while the comics push his world in bold new directions, from career-best character work to reality-spanning epics. This era refreshes his mythology for new generations across film, games, and a revitalized comics line.

arcs.title: Coming Home
arcs.logline: A mysterious totemic predator forces Spider-Man to question the true nature of his powers.
arcs.summary:
J. Michael Straczynski's celebrated run opens with the arrival of Ezekiel, an older man with spider-powers like Peter's, who warns that the radioactive spider may have been merely a vessel for something far older and more mystical. He brings a warning of Morlun, an immortal hunter who feeds on totemic beings.

The arc reinvigorated the franchise at the turn of the millennium, blending street-level humanity with a mythic new dimension to Spider-Man's origin and pairing Peter's heroics with the warmth of his rekindled marriage and a new teaching career.

With painterly art by John Romita Jr., 'Coming Home' is a fan-favorite modern starting point that respects the character's history while opening fresh storytelling doors.

reading_entries.title: The Amazing Spider-Man Vol. 1: Coming Home

arcs.title: Spider-Verse
arcs.logline: Every Spider-Man from every reality must unite against a family that devours them.
arcs.summary:
The vampiric Inheritors, led by Morlun and his kin, begin hunting and consuming Spider-Totems across the multiverse, threatening to wipe out every spider-powered hero in existence. To survive, Peter Parker joins forces with an army of alternate Spider-Men, Spider-Women, and even stranger arachnid heroes drawn from countless dimensions.

Dan Slott's sprawling event introduces fan-favorite characters and celebrates the limitless variety of the Spider concept, gathering wall-crawlers from comics, cartoons, and beyond into a single desperate war. It became a defining touchstone of the modern Spider-Man brand and directly inspired the acclaimed animated films.

Epic in scope yet anchored by Peter's leadership, 'Spider-Verse' is the definitive celebration of the idea that anyone can wear the mask.

reading_entries.title: Spider-Verse
reading_entries.issue_range: Amazing Spider-Man (2014) #7-15, Edge of Spider-Verse #1-5, and tie-ins

start_here.label: Absolute Beginner
start_here.reason: The complete, self-contained origin in a single issue. Read how Peter gains his powers and learns the lesson that makes him a hero, with no prior knowledge required.

start_here.label: Classic Fan
start_here.reason: A standalone Bronze Age masterpiece that shows Spider-Man at his most tested. You can read it cold and understand exactly why it tops 'greatest stories' lists.

start_here.label: Modern Era
start_here.reason: The multiverse epic that launched the modern Spider brand and inspired the animated films. A thrilling entry point into today's Spider-Man mythology.

relationships.summary: A mentor figure who saw Peter's potential and helped equip him for bigger threats.
relationships.notes: Tony Stark recruited and outfitted Spider-Man during the 'Civil War' era and acted as his mentor in the Marvel Cinematic Universe, building him advanced suits and a place on the Avengers' radar.

relationships.summary: A moral compass and fellow Avenger whom Spider-Man deeply admires.
relationships.notes: The two fought side by side in the Avengers and on opposite sides of 'Civil War' over the Superhuman Registration Act, with Cap's example pushing Peter to take a public stand.

relationships.summary: An unlikely, bickering friendship forged through repeated team-ups.
relationships.notes: The mismatched pair headlined the long-running 'Astonishing Spider-Man & Wolverine' team-ups, their odd-couple banter becoming a fan-favorite dynamic across Avengers stories.

relationships.summary: Spider-Man finally graduated from solo street hero to full Avenger.
relationships.notes: After decades on the sidelines, Spider-Man joined the team during Brian Michael Bendis's 'New Avengers' run, bringing his street-level perspective to Earth's Mightiest Heroes.

relationships.summary: His most personal nemesis, who knows his identity and has hurt those closest to him.
relationships.notes: Norman Osborn discovered Spider-Man's identity, killed Gwen Stacy in 'The Night Gwen Stacy Died,' and has tormented Peter across generations as the architect of his greatest losses.

relationships.summary: A brilliant scientist whose mechanical arms make him one of Spider-Man's deadliest foes.
relationships.notes: Otto Octavius orchestrated the 'Master Planner' saga and once swapped minds with Peter to become the 'Superior Spider-Man,' making him both intellectual rival and physical match.

relationships.summary: An alien symbiote bonded to a man who hates Peter, twisting Spider-Man's own image against him.
relationships.notes: After Spider-Man rejected the alien costume, it bonded with Eddie Brock to become Venom, a dark mirror immune to his spider-sense who knows his secret identity.

relationships.summary: A shape-shifting petty criminal able to transform his body into living sand.
relationships.notes: Flint Marko gained sand-based powers in a nuclear test accident and became an early recurring foe, clashing with Spider-Man solo and as a member of the Sinister Six and Frightful Four.

media.title: Spider-Man: The Animated Series
media.notes: The definitive 1990s cartoon that adapted classic storylines for a generation of fans.

media.title: Spectacular Spider-Man
media.notes: A critically beloved series praised for its sharp characterization and balance of high school drama and action.

media.title: Spider-Man
media.notes: Sam Raimi's landmark film, starring Tobey Maguire, that helped launch the modern superhero movie era.

media.title: Spider-Man: Into the Spider-Verse
media.notes: Academy Award winner for Best Animated Feature, introducing Miles Morales and a multiverse of Spider-People.

media.title: Marvel's Spider-Man
media.notes: Insomniac Games' acclaimed open-world adventure praised for its fluid web-swinging and emotional story.

entities.name: Spike
entities.short_bio: Spike is a brash, punk-rock vampire who revels in violence and the legend of killing Slayers. Charismatic and unpredictable, his shifting loyalties make him as likely to fight beside Buffy as against her.

eras.title: Slayer of Slayers
eras.summary: Spike storms into Sunnydale chasing the thrill of killing a Slayer, his motives and allegiances forever in flux.

arcs.title: The Big Bad
arcs.logline: A notorious vampire rolls into town hunting the reputation a Slayer can give him.
arcs.summary:
Spike arrives in Sunnydale drawn by the challenge Buffy represents, eager to add a third Slayer to his kill count. Reckless and theatrical, he upends the local balance of power.

Yet his bravado masks a more complicated nature, and his alliances shift with his appetites. Spike's arc keeps readers guessing whether he is a deadly foe or an unlikely asset.

reading_entries.title: Buffy the Vampire Slayer Vol. 3: From Beneath You

relationships.summary: A swaggering vampire drawn to Sunnydale by the thrill of killing a Slayer.
relationships.notes: Spike begins as a lethal antagonist relishing the hunt, his loyalties toward Buffy always in flux.

media.title: Buffy the Vampire Slayer
media.notes: Spike evolves from villain to complex antihero across the series.

media.title: Angel
media.notes: Spike joins the cast of the spin-off in its final season.

entities.name: Starfire
entities.short_bio: Koriand'r, called Starfire, is a warrior princess from the alien world of Tamaran who can fly and channel solar energy into devastating starbolts. Passionate, fierce and warm-hearted, she became a defining member of the New Teen Titans. Her romance with Dick Grayson is one of the franchise's most celebrated relationships.

eras.title: The Tamaranean Princess
eras.summary: Fleeing slavery and her homeworld's politics, Starfire finds a family among the New Teen Titans and becomes one of their fiercest and most beloved members.

arcs.title: Arrival on Earth
arcs.logline: An alien princess escapes captivity and finds a new home among Earth's young heroes.
arcs.summary:
Starfire crash-lands into the lives of the New Teen Titans while fleeing alien captors, quickly proving herself a powerful ally and devoted friend. Her arrival adds a cosmic dimension and emotional warmth to the team.

The arc establishes her backstory, powers and the deep bonds that make her central to the Wolfman-Perez era.

reading_entries.title: The New Teen Titans Omnibus Vol. 1

relationships.summary: Starfire is a core member of the New Teen Titans and one of its emotional anchors.
relationships.notes: Her loyalty and warmth define the team's found-family dynamic.

media.title: Teen Titans
media.notes: Starfire is a lead character across the series.

media.title: Teen Titans Go!
media.notes: A comedic version of Starfire anchors the ensemble.

media.title: Titans
media.notes: Anna Diop stars as Kory Anders / Starfire.

entities.name: Starro
entities.short_bio: Starro is a colossal, telepathic alien conqueror resembling a starfish, capable of subjugating entire populations by attaching spore-like duplicates to their faces. As the first villain the Justice League ever fought, Starro is a signature recurring menace symbolizing planetary-scale threats.

eras.title: The First Conqueror
eras.summary: Debuting as the threat that united the League, Starro evolves from Silver Age oddity into a genuinely menacing cosmic conqueror whose mind-control spores threaten worlds.

arcs.title: The Star Conqueror
arcs.logline: A telepathic alien starfish returns to enslave humanity on a planetary scale.
arcs.summary:
Starro descends on Earth with vastly expanded ambitions, deploying countless spore-duplicates to seize control of entire populations. Its telepathic dominion threatens to turn humanity into a single enslaved hive.

The story escalates the original Silver Age threat into a credible world-ending menace, reinforcing why the League formed in the first place. Resistance requires both power and ingenuity.

The heroes must free the minds of the enslaved while confronting the central intelligence directing the swarm. The scale underscores Starro's status as a foundational League foe.

The arc reaffirms Starro as an enduring symbol of the cosmic threats the team exists to repel.

reading_entries.title: Justice League of America: The Second Coming

relationships.summary: The mind-controlling alien the League first assembled to defeat.
relationships.notes: The team's very first foe and a recurring planetary threat.

media.title: The Suicide Squad
media.notes: Featured as the towering alien antagonist Starro the Conqueror.

media.title: Batman: The Brave and the Bold
media.notes: Appears as a classic alien menace.

media.title: DC Universe Online
media.notes: Featured as an alien invasion threat.

entities.name: Storm
entities.short_bio: Storm is a weather-controlling mutant once worshipped as a goddess, who became one of the X-Men's most powerful and respected members. Regal and compassionate, she has led the team through some of its hardest trials. Her command of the elements and her natural authority make her a pillar of the franchise.

eras.title: Goddess of the Storm
eras.summary: Recruited in the all-new roster, Storm rose quickly from member to leader, guiding the X-Men through landmark adventures.

arcs.title: The Goddess Leads
arcs.logline: A weather goddess sheds her past to take command of the X-Men.
arcs.summary:
Ororo Munroe joins the all-new X-Men and soon proves herself one of their most capable members. When circumstances demand it, she steps into leadership, guiding the team with wisdom and resolve.

Her journey from worshipped goddess to grounded leader is among the most celebrated character arcs of the Claremont era.

It establishes Storm as an enduring symbol of strength and dignity.

reading_entries.title: Marvel Masterworks: The Uncanny X-Men Vol. 1

start_here.label: Witness the goddess rise
start_here.reason: Storm's introduction and ascent in the all-new roster showcase why she is a fan favorite.

relationships.summary: Storm is a longtime core member and leader of the X-Men.
relationships.notes: One of the team's most powerful and respected figures.

media.title: X-Men
media.notes: Halle Berry portrays Storm in the original film trilogy.

media.title: X-Men: The Animated Series
media.notes: A prominent and beloved member of the animated lineup.

media.title: X-Men '97
media.notes: Central to the emotional arcs of the revival.

entities.name: Superman
entities.short_bio: Rocketed to Earth as an infant from the dying planet Krypton, Kal-El was raised in rural Kansas as Clark Kent and grew into the world's first and greatest super-hero. Empowered by the light of Earth's yellow sun, he wields incredible strength, flight, and invulnerability in the service of truth and justice. Behind the cape he is a mild-mannered Metropolis reporter whose deepest power is an unshakable belief in the good of others.

eras.title: Golden Age (1938-1955)
eras.summary: The original crusading Superman of the Depression era, a champion of the oppressed who battled crooked landlords and war profiteers as much as costumed villains. These early stories defined the template for every super-hero that followed.

arcs.title: The Coming of Superman
arcs.logline: A strange visitor from another planet becomes Earth's first super-hero in the very first comic-book adventures of Superman.
arcs.summary:
Jerry Siegel and Joe Shuster's debut tales introduce the Man of Steel as a two-fisted reformer who can leap eighth-of-a-mile, hurdle skyscrapers, and outrun a locomotive. In his first outings he saves an innocent woman from the electric chair, terrorizes a wife-beater, and exposes a corrupt senator, establishing a Superman driven by social conscience rather than spectacle.

These formative stories also build the supporting cast and dual identity that would endure for nearly a century. Clark Kent takes a job at the Daily Star (later the Daily Planet), competes with the fearless reporter Lois Lane, and conceals his strength behind a timid newsroom persona.

The scope of his abilities grows quickly across the early issues, from prodigious leaps to true flight, but the core ethic remains constant: Superman uses his gifts to protect ordinary people from those who would prey on them.

reading_entries.title: Superman: The Golden Age Omnibus, Vol. 1

arcs.title: Enter the Imp and the Mad Scientist
arcs.logline: Superman faces his earliest recurring foes, from a brilliant criminal scientist to a reality-warping prankster from the Fifth Dimension.
arcs.summary:
As the feature matured, Siegel and Shuster gave Superman his first true rogues. A red-headed criminal genius (soon reimagined as the bald Lex Luthor) emerges as the Man of Steel's intellectual opposite, scheming for power from the shadows.

The era also introduces Mr. Mxyzptlk, a mischievous imp from the Fifth Dimension whose magical pranks can only be undone by tricking him into saying his own name backward. These tales push Superman beyond crime-busting into fantastical, imaginative territory.

Together these stories expand the Superman mythos from grounded social drama toward the larger-than-life adventure that would dominate the decades to come.

reading_entries.title: Superman: The Golden Age Omnibus, Vol. 2

eras.title: Silver & Bronze Age (1956-1985)
eras.summary: An age of cosmic wonder that built out the full Superman mythology: Krypton, the bottle city of Kandor, the Phantom Zone, and a sprawling Superman Family. Writers piled on imaginative concepts before the era closed with a loving send-off to the classic continuity.

arcs.title: The Bottle City of Kandor & the Last Days of Krypton
arcs.logline: Superman discovers a shrunken Kryptonian city stolen by Brainiac, and with it a living link to the homeworld he never knew.
arcs.summary:
Otto Binder, Curt Swan, and their collaborators deepened Superman's connection to his lost heritage. The android villain Brainiac is revealed to have miniaturized and bottled the Kryptonian capital of Kandor before the planet's destruction, leaving Superman to safeguard the last surviving city of his people in his Fortress of Solitude.

The period fleshes out Krypton itself, from the doomed scientist Jor-El to the crystalline science of a vanished civilization. Superman's vow to one day restore Kandor to full size becomes a recurring emotional throughline.

These stories transform Superman from a costumed strongman into the heir of an entire alien culture, grounding his power in tragedy and responsibility.

reading_entries.title: Superman: The Silver Age Omnibus, Vol. 1

arcs.title: Kryptonite Nevermore
arcs.logline: When all the kryptonite on Earth is rendered inert, a strange sand-doppelganger threatens to drain Superman's very existence.
arcs.summary:
Denny O'Neil and Curt Swan opened the Bronze Age with a bold attempt to modernize Superman by removing his over-reliance on kryptonite as a plot crutch. A laboratory accident transmutes all kryptonite into ordinary iron, but the same event spawns a sand creature that mirrors Superman and slowly siphons away his power.

The story experiments with a more grounded, vulnerable Man of Steel and even sees Clark Kent shift careers from print journalism to television news at WGBS. It reflects an industry-wide push to make its icons feel relevant to a changing readership.

Though later writers reintroduced kryptonite, this arc remains a landmark example of creators wrestling with how to keep a near-omnipotent hero dramatically compelling.

arcs.title: Whatever Happened to the Man of Tomorrow?
arcs.logline: Alan Moore writes the final, definitive farewell to the Silver Age Superman as his greatest enemies make one last devastating push.
arcs.summary:
As DC prepared to reboot its universe, Alan Moore and Curt Swan crafted an imaginary 'last' Superman story framed as a tale told years after the hero's disappearance. The Man of Steel's rogues' gallery, manipulated by a mind behind the scenes, launches a final all-out assault on everyone he loves, forcing him to retreat to the Fortress of Solitude.

Moore treats the classic continuity with deep affection, weaving together Krypto, the bottle city, Mr. Mxyzptlk, and the entire Superman Family into a poignant, bittersweet climax. It is widely regarded as one of the finest Superman stories ever written.

The arc closes the door on three decades of imaginative Silver and Bronze Age lore, clearing the stage for John Byrne's modern reinvention.
arcs.spoiler: The story is framed as Superman's final adventure, culminating in his apparent retirement from the Superman identity.

reading_entries.title: Superman: Whatever Happened to the Man of Tomorrow?

eras.title: Modern Age (1986-2010)
eras.summary: John Byrne's post-Crisis reboot streamlined Superman into a more human, contemporary figure, setting the stage for the era's defining epics. The period delivered the most famous Superman stories of the modern age, from his death to his marriage.

arcs.title: The Man of Steel
arcs.logline: John Byrne rebuilds Superman from the ground up for a new generation, retelling his origin and reintroducing his world.
arcs.summary:
Following Crisis on Infinite Earths, John Byrne was given the keys to streamline and modernize Superman in a six-issue miniseries. He restores Krypton as a cold, sterile civilization, emphasizes the Kents' lifelong influence on Clark, and reframes Superman as a self-made hero who discovered his powers gradually rather than as a super-baby.

The series also reinvents Lex Luthor as a corrupt corporate billionaire rather than a mad scientist, and recasts Lois Lane as a fierce investigative rival. It strips away decades of accumulated continuity to present a leaner, more relatable Man of Steel.

This reboot governed Superman's characterization for two decades and remains the foundation of most modern interpretations.

reading_entries.title: Superman: The Man of Steel, Vol. 1

arcs.title: The Death of Superman
arcs.logline: An unstoppable engine of destruction called Doomsday cuts a path of carnage toward Metropolis, and only Superman stands in its way.
arcs.summary:
In the era's defining blockbuster, a monstrous creature claws its way out of an underground prison and rampages across America with mindless, escalating fury. The Justice League is brushed aside, and Superman alone is left to halt the beast before it reaches Metropolis.

The ensuing brawl is a brutal, city-leveling slugfest that pushes both combatants past their limits. The story became a global media phenomenon and one of the best-selling comics of all time.

Its aftermath fractured Superman's world and set up the sprawling 'World Without a Superman' and 'Reign of the Supermen' sagas that followed.
arcs.spoiler: Superman and Doomsday strike simultaneous killing blows; Superman dies in Lois Lane's arms before later returning.

reading_entries.title: The Death of Superman
reading_entries.issue_range: Superman #73-75, Justice League America #69, and tie-ins

reading_entries.title: Superman: The Death and Return of Superman Omnibus
reading_entries.issue_range: The full Death, World Without a Superman, and Reign of the Supermen saga

arcs.title: All-Star Superman
arcs.logline: Dying from solar radiation poisoning, Superman sets out to complete twelve final labors and leave the world ready to live without him.
arcs.summary:
Grant Morrison and Frank Quitely's twelve-issue masterpiece distills the entire Superman mythos into a single luminous story. After Lex Luthor's scheme overloads Superman's cells with sunlight, the hero learns his powers are increasing even as his body burns out, granting him a finite window to set his affairs in order.

Freed from continuity, Morrison celebrates every facet of the legend: Lois Lane, Jimmy Olsen, Krypto, the Bizarro world, the bottle city, and a final reckoning with Luthor. Each chapter is a self-contained myth that reaffirms Superman's boundless kindness.

Widely hailed as one of the greatest Superman stories ever told, it presents the character as humanity's gentle, hopeful ideal made flesh.

eras.title: Rebirth & Beyond (2011-Present)
eras.summary: After the New 52 reboot and the Rebirth initiative, Superman was restored as a married father, redefining the icon through family and legacy. Modern runs explore what it means for the Man of Tomorrow to raise the next generation.

arcs.title: Son of Superman
arcs.logline: The classic married Superman returns to a world that has lost its own, raising his young son Jonathan while shouldering the cape once more.
arcs.summary:
Peter J. Tomasi and Patrick Gleason relaunched the title with the pre-Flashpoint Superman, now a husband to Lois and father to a powered young son, Jonathan Kent. Settling on a farm and re-entering a world without its native Superman, Clark must balance heroism with the very human work of fatherhood.

The run leans into warmth and family adventure as Jon's emerging abilities draw the attention of cosmic threats. It reframes Superman's heroism as something he passes on rather than performs alone.

This era reestablished the married Superman fans had missed and launched Jonathan Kent as the next Superboy.

arcs.title: Superman: Up in the Sky
arcs.logline: Superman crosses the galaxy on a desperate, soul-testing quest to rescue a single abducted child.
arcs.summary:
Tom King and Andy Kubert deliver an introspective road-trip epic in which Superman is asked whether he would travel to the far end of the universe to save one lost girl, even if it means abandoning Earth for an uncertain time. He says yes without hesitation, and the journey becomes a meditation on what makes him a hero.

Each chapter is a parable testing Superman's compassion, judgment, and limits against impossible odds. The story argues that his greatness lies not in his power but in his refusal to ever stop caring about the individual.

Originally serialized in the oversized Superman: Up in the Sky, it stands as a modern showcase for the character's moral core.

start_here.label: Absolute Beginner
start_here.reason: John Byrne's six-issue reboot is the cleanest modern jumping-on point, retelling Superman's origin and introducing his entire world from scratch with no prior reading required.

start_here.label: Classic Fan
start_here.reason: The Death of Superman is the most famous Superman story ever published and captures the high-stakes, blockbuster spirit of his classic adventures against an unstoppable foe.

start_here.label: Modern Era
start_here.reason: All-Star Superman is a self-contained, continuity-free masterpiece that distills everything great about the character into twelve perfect issues, ideal for any modern reader.

relationships.summary: Superman's closest and most complementary friend, the World's Finest pairing of brightest hope and darkest vigilance.
relationships.notes: Their dynamic anchors stories like The World's Finest and Jeph Loeb's Superman/Batman run; Batman famously keeps a kryptonite contingency should Superman ever fall.

relationships.summary: A fellow founding pillar of the heroic community whom Superman regards as a peer in both power and principle.
relationships.notes: The two have stood together since the Justice League's founding and were briefly romantically paired during the New 52 era's Superman/Wonder Woman series.

relationships.summary: A trusted Justice League teammate whose friendly speed rivalry with Superman is a long-running fan favorite.
relationships.notes: Their good-natured charity races, dating back to the Silver Age, are a recurring tradition across comics and animation.

relationships.summary: A dependable Justice League ally who guards Earth's sector of the cosmos alongside Superman.
relationships.notes: Hal Jordan and Superman have fought side by side since the League's earliest cases, balancing willpower-forged constructs against raw Kryptonian might.

relationships.summary: The world's premier super-team, of which Superman is a founding member and moral cornerstone.
relationships.notes: From the Justice League of America through the JLA and Rebirth eras, Superman is consistently portrayed as the team's heart and most trusted leader.

relationships.summary: Superman's greatest nemesis, a brilliant Metropolis billionaire who hates him out of wounded pride and jealous ambition.
relationships.notes: Reinvented by John Byrne in The Man of Steel as a corrupt corporate magnate, Luthor's obsession drives stories from All-Star Superman to countless schemes against the Man of Steel.

relationships.summary: A coldly logical alien intelligence who collects and bottles entire cities, including the last surviving Kryptonian capital.
relationships.notes: Brainiac's theft of Kandor before Krypton's destruction makes him a living link to Superman's lost heritage, central to Geoff Johns's modern Brainiac arc.

relationships.summary: A ruthless Kryptonian military commander who shares Superman's powers but none of his compassion.
relationships.notes: Banished to the Phantom Zone before Krypton's fall, Zod escapes to challenge Superman as his equal in strength, a conflict explored in stories like New Krypton.

relationships.summary: A mindless engineered killing machine that evolves to overcome any threat, and the only foe ever to slay Superman in single combat.
relationships.notes: Doomsday's debut rampage in The Death of Superman ended with both combatants striking simultaneous killing blows.

media.title: Superman: The Animated Series
media.notes: Bruce Timm and Paul Dini's acclaimed series that became a cornerstone of the DC Animated Universe.

media.title: Smallville
media.notes: A ten-season series chronicling Clark Kent's coming-of-age in Kansas before he became Superman.

media.title: Superman: The Movie
media.notes: Richard Donner's landmark film starring Christopher Reeve that proved a comic-book hero could carry a blockbuster.

media.title: Injustice 2
media.notes: A fighting game where Superman is a central figure in a story about heroes divided over how far to go to protect the world.

entities.name: Surtur
entities.short_bio: Surtur is the colossal fire demon of Muspelheim, a primordial force of destruction destined to bring about Ragnarok. Towering and near-omnipotent, he wields the flaming Twilight Sword and exists to burn the Nine Realms to cinders, making him among the most apocalyptic threats Thor and Asgard have ever faced.

eras.title: Herald of Ragnarok
eras.summary: Introduced as a looming doom in the Silver Age, Surtur reaches his fullest realization as the engine of apocalypse in the Simonson era.

arcs.title: Doom of the Gods
arcs.logline: Surtur forges the Twilight Sword and marches on Asgard to ignite Ragnarok.
arcs.summary:
After centuries of imprisonment, Surtur completes the Twilight Sword and sets out to fulfill the prophecy of Ragnarok, the fiery doom of the gods. His coming is felt across the Nine Realms as an unstoppable apocalypse.

Thor, Odin, and the defenders of Asgard must unite in a desperate last stand against a foe whose power dwarfs even the All-Father's. Walter Simonson stages the conflict on a truly cosmic, operatic scale.

The story is the definitive Surtur tale and one of the greatest Thor epics ever told, establishing the fire demon as the ultimate harbinger of the end times.

start_here.label: Classic Fan
start_here.reason: Doom of the Gods is the definitive Surtur story and the climax of the legendary Simonson run.

relationships.summary: Surtur exists to destroy Asgard and bring about Ragnarok, forcing Thor into apocalyptic last stands.
relationships.notes: His forging of the Twilight Sword drove the climactic Surtur Saga in Walter Simonson's run.

media.title: Thor: Ragnarok
media.notes: Surtur appears as the fire demon whose prophecy of Ragnarok is central to the film's climax.

media.title: LEGO Marvel Super Heroes
media.notes: Surtur appears among the roster of Marvel villains in this action-adventure game.

entities.name: Teen Titans
entities.short_bio: The Teen Titans are DC Comics' premier team of young heroes, originally formed by the teenage sidekicks of the Justice League and reinvented across generations. United by friendship as much as duty, they balance world-saving adventures with the growing pains of adolescence. Over the decades the roster has shifted dramatically, but the team remains DC's enduring symbol of heroes coming of age.

eras.title: Silver Age Origins
eras.summary: The team debuts as a gathering of Justice League sidekicks who realize they work best together. Across early try-out stories and their first ongoing series, Robin, Kid Flash, Aqualad and Wonder Girl establish the Teen Titans as a distinct franchise aimed at younger readers, eventually adding Speedy to the lineup.

arcs.title: The Brave and the Bold Debut
arcs.logline: Three teenage sidekicks team up for the first time to solve a crisis their mentors are too busy to handle.
arcs.summary:
The concept of the Teen Titans first crystallizes when Robin, Kid Flash and Aqualad answer a distress call in a small town, working as a unit without their adult mentors. The young heroes discover that their combined skills and easy camaraderie make them formidable in their own right.

The try-out story tests whether DC's sidekick characters could carry adventures on their own. Its success convinced editors to keep the trio together and expand the concept.

The debut establishes the core appeal that would define the franchise for decades: heroes young enough to relate to the readers, facing threats scaled to their experience while learning to trust one another. It plants the seeds for a full team.

reading_entries.title: Teen Titans: The Silver Age Omnibus
reading_entries.issue_range: The Brave and the Bold #54, #60; Showcase #59

arcs.title: The First Ongoing Series
arcs.logline: Now a full team with a name, the young heroes take on mod villains and youth-culture crises of the 1960s.
arcs.summary:
With Wonder Girl joining and the team officially christened, the Teen Titans launch their own title and quickly become DC's voice for 1960s youth culture. Stories often pit the heroes against threats aimed squarely at teenagers, with the Titans frequently acting as mediators between the adult world and their own generation.

Speedy rotates in and out of the roster, and the team develops a clubhouse, a distinct slang-heavy voice, and a rogues' gallery of their own. The book leans into mod fashion, social causes and a lighter tone than the adult Justice League.

Though rooted in its era, this run cements the Titans as a permanent fixture of the DC Universe and gives later writers a foundation of relationships to build on.

reading_entries.title: Teen Titans: The Silver Age Omnibus

eras.title: The New Teen Titans
eras.summary: Marv Wolfman and George Perez relaunch the team with a new lineup that becomes one of DC's best-selling and most acclaimed series. Adding Starfire, Raven and Cyborg to returning members, the era is defined by deep character drama, iconic villains, and landmark storylines that rival the X-Men for soap-operatic ambition.

arcs.title: The Judas Contract
arcs.logline: The mercenary Deathstroke closes in on the Titans with the help of a new recruit who is not what she seems.
arcs.summary:
Slade Wilson, the assassin Deathstroke, has inherited a contract on the Teen Titans, and he executes it through a long con that strikes at the heart of the team's trust. The storyline pays off subplots seeded across years of issues, building to a betrayal that reshapes the group.

Widely regarded as the definitive Teen Titans story, The Judas Contract showcases Wolfman and Perez at their peak, weaving espionage, tragedy and personal stakes into a tight, character-driven arc. It also introduces a pivotal new hero in the process.

The arc's emotional fallout, betrayal, loss and hard-won growth, defines the modern team and establishes Deathstroke as the Titans' signature antagonist for generations to come.
arcs.spoiler: Terra is revealed to be a double agent working for Deathstroke all along, and she dies during the climactic confrontation.

reading_entries.title: The New Teen Titans: The Judas Contract
reading_entries.issue_range: Tales of the Teen Titans #42-44 & Annual #3

arcs.title: The Terror of Trigon
arcs.logline: Raven's demonic father makes his bid to conquer Earth, and the Titans must save their teammate's soul.
arcs.summary:
The interdimensional demon Trigon, Raven's monstrous father, finally moves to claim Earth and corrupt his daughter. Having been founded in part to oppose him, the New Teen Titans face their most cosmic and personal threat as Raven's heritage threatens to consume her.

The arc deepens Raven's tragic backstory and tests the bonds of the team against a foe of nearly limitless power. It blends horror, mythology and the close-knit found-family dynamic that defines the Wolfman-Perez run.

Resolving long-running threads about Raven's origin and the cult that birthed her, the storyline is a cornerstone of the era and a showcase for one of the team's most enduring characters.

reading_entries.title: The New Teen Titans: The Terror of Trigon

arcs.title: The Cult of Brother Blood
arcs.logline: A charismatic immortal cult leader sets his deadly church against the Titans.
arcs.summary:
Brother Blood, the latest in a centuries-long line of cult patriarchs sustained by blood rituals, draws the Teen Titans into conflict as his fanatical Church of Blood spreads its influence. When a member of the team becomes entangled with the cult, the Titans must confront an enemy who weaponizes faith and devotion.

The storyline expands the New Teen Titans' rogues' gallery with a genuinely sinister, ideologically driven villain, contrasting the team's youthful idealism against organized fanaticism.

Brother Blood becomes a recurring nemesis across the era, and these early confrontations establish the menace that later incarnations of the team, including the animated series, would draw upon.

reading_entries.title: The New Teen Titans Omnibus Vol. 1

eras.title: Modern Titans
eras.summary: A new generation takes the spotlight as Geoff Johns relaunches the team for the 2000s, bridging the original sidekicks with younger heroes. Through the Johns era, the New 52 reboot, and Rebirth, the Teen Titans continually reinvent themselves while honoring their legacy and mentorship themes.

arcs.title: A Kid's Game
arcs.logline: A new Teen Titans assembles as veteran members mentor a fresh class of young heroes.
arcs.summary:
In the wake of tragedy, the surviving original Titans decide to pay forward what they learned by mentoring a new generation. Geoff Johns and Mike McKone relaunch the franchise with a lineup that pairs grown-up legacy heroes with younger members, reframing the team around teaching and inheritance.

The opening arc quickly establishes the new dynamic and throws the rebuilt team against a familiar, formidable threat that tests whether the kids are ready. It re-centers the book on the mentorship themes at the heart of the Titans concept.

This run revitalizes the franchise for a new decade, blending nostalgia for the Wolfman-Perez era with a forward-looking cast and setting up years of acclaimed stories.

arcs.title: The Future Is Now
arcs.logline: A glimpse of a dark possible future forces the young Titans to confront what they might become.
arcs.summary:
The team is hurled into a grim future where their grown-up selves have become tyrants, confronting the heroes with the worst version of their own potential. The story raises the stakes of the Johns run and ties the new Titans into the broader fabric of the DC Universe's timeline.

The arc deepens characterization for the younger members, forcing each to reckon with destiny, legacy and the choices that separate a hero from a villain. It also reinforces the mentorship throughline as veterans try to steer the kids away from a dark path.

A fan-favorite storyline of the era, it demonstrates how the modern Titans book balanced superheroics with the introspective coming-of-age drama that defines the franchise.

reading_entries.title: Teen Titans: The Future Is Now

start_here.label: Start with the definitive Titans story
start_here.reason: The Judas Contract is the single most acclaimed Teen Titans story and the perfect showcase of the team's relationships, stakes and signature villain.

start_here.label: Meet the modern team
start_here.reason: A Kid's Game is the most accessible modern jumping-on point, reintroducing the franchise with clear mentorship themes for new readers.

start_here.label: Understand Raven and Trigon
start_here.reason: The Terror of Trigon delivers the team's most personal cosmic conflict and the backstory that anchors the popular animated incarnations.

relationships.summary: The Titans began as the proteges of Justice League members and remain the League's most trusted younger counterpart.
relationships.notes: Many Titans graduate into Justice League membership, reinforcing the mentorship legacy between the two teams.

relationships.summary: The Flash's legacy is woven into the Titans through Kid Flash, a founding member, making the speedster a longtime ally and mentor figure.
relationships.notes: Wally West served on the Titans for years before inheriting the Flash mantle.

relationships.summary: As the team's original leader, Dick Grayson is the heart and soul of the Titans across nearly every incarnation.
relationships.notes: His leadership defines the team even after he becomes Nightwing.

relationships.summary: Cyborg is one of the New Teen Titans' founding modern members and a steadfast pillar of the team.
relationships.notes: His tenure with the Titans long predates his later Justice League membership.

relationships.summary: Deathstroke the Terminator is the Teen Titans' signature archenemy, a master assassin who has hunted them for decades.
relationships.notes: His vendetta drives The Judas Contract and countless subsequent conflicts.

relationships.summary: The interdimensional demon Trigon is Raven's father and one of the most powerful cosmic threats the team has ever faced.
relationships.notes: The team was partly assembled to oppose his invasion of Earth.

relationships.summary: Brother Blood is an immortal cult leader whose Church of Blood repeatedly menaces the Titans.
relationships.notes: He is a recurring ideological villain across multiple eras and the animated series.

relationships.summary: Terra infiltrated the team as a recruit while secretly serving Deathstroke, becoming one of comics' most infamous traitors.
relationships.notes: Her betrayal is the emotional core of The Judas Contract.

media.title: Teen Titans
media.notes: The beloved anime-influenced series centered on Robin, Starfire, Raven, Cyborg and Beast Boy.

media.title: Teen Titans Go!
media.notes: A comedic, slice-of-life reimagining of the 2003 cast that became a long-running hit.

media.title: Titans
media.notes: A darker, mature live-action series following Dick Grayson and a new generation of Titans.

media.title: Teen Titans: The Judas Contract
media.notes: An animated film adaptation of the landmark comic storyline.

team_members.notes: The original Robin and founding leader; later operates as Nightwing while still anchoring the team.
Kid Flash (Wally West) was a founding member before inheriting the Flash mantle.
The Tamaranean princess Koriand'r joined in the New Teen Titans era and became a core member.
The empath who assembled the New Teen Titans to oppose her father Trigon.
Victor Stone was a founding member of the New Teen Titans long before joining the Justice League.
Formerly Changeling; a fan-favorite shapeshifter and the team's comic relief.

entities.name: Terra
entities.short_bio: Tara Markov, the geokinetic hero called Terra, can move and reshape earth and stone with her mind. Introduced as a promising new Titans recruit, she became one of comics' most notorious figures for her hidden allegiances. Her story is inseparable from the team's most famous storyline.

eras.title: The Recruit
eras.summary: Terra joins the New Teen Titans as a powerful young earth-mover, quickly becoming entangled in the events leading to The Judas Contract.

arcs.title: The Judas Contract
arcs.logline: A new recruit's hidden loyalties bring the team to the brink of ruin.
arcs.summary:
Terra's tenure with the Titans builds toward the devastating revelations of The Judas Contract, in which her true allegiance reshapes the team forever. Her arc is a masterclass in long-form character subterfuge.

The storyline remains one of the most discussed in DC history, and Terra's role in it has defined every subsequent interpretation of the character.
arcs.spoiler: Terra is secretly a deep-cover agent for Deathstroke and dies during the storyline's climax.

reading_entries.title: The New Teen Titans: The Judas Contract
reading_entries.issue_range: Tales of the Teen Titans #42-44 & Annual #3

relationships.summary: Terra infiltrated the Teen Titans while secretly working against them, becoming an infamous traitor.
relationships.notes: Her betrayal is the emotional core of The Judas Contract.

media.title: Teen Titans
media.notes: Terra is featured prominently in the second season's central storyline.

media.title: Teen Titans: The Judas Contract
media.notes: Terra is a central character in the animated adaptation.

media.title: DC Universe Online
media.notes: Terra appears among the game's roster of DC characters.

entities.name: Terry Fitzgerald
entities.short_bio: Terry Fitzgerald was Al Simmons' closest friend in life and now leads a quiet family life with Al's widow, Wanda. Unaware of his connection to the creature haunting the city, he becomes an emotional anchor for Spawn's lingering humanity.

eras.title: The Life Left Behind
eras.summary: Terry represents the normal life Al lost, raising a family while danger from Al's past circles ever closer.

arcs.title: The Friend Who Remains
arcs.logline: A grieving best friend builds a new life unaware that his dead friend walks again.
arcs.summary:
